data.title: REIT To Avoid: 11% Yielding Community Healthcare Trust
data.description:
Big winners are exciting. Every investor loves to talk about their “double baggers” and home runs, but perhaps the more effective means of beating the market is the avoidance of bad investments.

At 2nd Market Capital, we emphasize digging into management teams, balance sheets, debt covenants, and any other subtle aspect that can cause a stock to implode. Avoiding disaster allows one’s winners to accrue toward steadily improving account value.

With that in mind, we built and continuously update a list of REITs to avoid. These are companies that appear opportunistic at first glance, but a deeper dive reveals significant risks to shareholder value. The full list on Portfolio Income Solutions currently consists of 14 REITs.

This article will focus specifically on Community Healthcare Trust Incorporated (CHCT).

The Temptation

CHCT looks tantalizing with an 11.15% dividend yield. It has a nearly perfect dividend history with continuous raises.

To top it all off, the dividend appears fully covered with an 88.4% AFFO payout ratio on 2025 AFFO.

However, we think a dividend cut is quite likely due to 5 key problems:

  1. Excessive G&A.
  2. True AFFO is much lower than reported AFFO.
  3. Tenant problems.
  4. Busted growth model.
  5. Mounting debt load.

Excessive G&A

2025 G&A was $25.095 million.

This is a company with a market cap of $460 million, so G&A in a single year was 5.4% of market cap. 2025 rental revenue was $121.351 million, so G&A was almost 21% of revenue.

That level of overhead expense is so hard to overcome. It is imperative that small REITs operate with extreme efficiency. CHCT fails to do this, instead electing to pay executives large salaries and bonuses. We had hoped the company learned its lesson after the egregious $24.7 million former CEO Timothy Wallace received in 2023.

Unfortunately, really high G&A resurfaced in 2025.

True AFFO Does Not Cover The Dividend

CHCT’s reported AFFO is consistently higher than its FFO.

AFFO is generally going to be lower than FFO, especially in a high-capex asset class like healthcare. AFFO being higher should immediately raise red flags and encourage digging into the accounting.

Here is the reconciliation of FFO to AFFO.

A whopping $14.896 million stock comp was added back to AFFO in 2025. With 27.259 million shares outstanding, that is fully $0.54 per share of stock comp add-backs.

So while the reported $2.16 AFFO in 2025 appears to cover the $1.91 dividend, true AFFO was only $1.62.

True AFFO payout ratio is 118%.

Tenant Problems

A tenant occupying 6 behavioral health facilities stopped paying full rent, resulting in significant impairment as described in an Earnings Report:

“During the three months ended June 30, 2025, the Company recorded a credit loss reserve on its notes related to a geriatric behavioral hospital tenant totaling approximately $8.7 million. During the three months ended June 30, 2024, the Company recorded an $11.0 million credit loss reserve related to its notes receivable with this geriatric behavioral hospital tenant. Because these notes are incidental to the Company's main business, the Company added back these reserves in its calculations of FFO and AFFO.”

This $8.7 million impairment was added back into FFO and AFFO. Inclusive of the impairment, AFFO would have been another $0.32 per share lower.

As described in our previous article on CHCT, they operate in a high-risk segment, so tenant problems are a higher-than-normal risk. The tradeoff inherent to their business model is that CHCT gets to acquire properties at very high cap rates (the positive) in exchange for the properties being higher risk due to some combination of location, tenant, type, and size.

CHCT was previously able to acquire in a highly accretive fashion as they traded at a very high multiple, which gave them cheap equity capital. The stock price has since collapsed, eliminating the opportunity to issue equity accretively.

Busted Business Model

With the equity faucet cut off, CHCT has had to shift to funding acquisitions with some combination of asset sales and debt.

Asset sales have high cap rates, making spreads on new acquisitions less accretive. To preserve accretion, CHCT seems to have engaged in significant debt issuance.

Debt ratios have increased considerably, with debt to enterprise value jumping to over 50%.

This alone would not be concerning.

REITs can change their debt level as a basic capital allocation decision. To the extent debt is measured to be the most effective source of capital, it can sometimes be the correct decision to lever up.

However, when a REIT levers up, it should result in significant AFFO/share accretion.

This is not the case for CHCT, which saw its AFFO/share falter at the same time debt ratios increased.

Higher debt and lower AFFO/share is a bad trajectory.

Dividend Outlook

If one properly adjusts for capex, impairments, and G&A, the dividend is not even close to being covered. CHCT can elect to keep paying it, but doing so would be financed by more debt, which is already becoming a problem.

Thus, I don’t know when the dividend cut will happen, but it seems inevitable to me unless there is a remarkable fundamental turnaround in which CHCT can suddenly lease up its vacant space.

Avoid Troubled REITs

When a company’s board signs off on massive pay packages for management while shareholders suffer, it is probably not a good place to invest. Operating efficiency remains one of the most important aspects of REIT profitability, especially for small caps. Don’t forget to do due diligence management before buying.

data.title: NFTY: Structural Headwinds Persist (Still On Hold)
data.description:
I last covered First Trust India NIFTY 50 Equal Weight ETF (NFTY) just two months prior and highlighted that even though the index appears to be relatively better, it still fails to deliver an adequate rate of return for investors. Since then, the fund is down by ~4.5%, underperforming the S&P 500 by around ~9%. That’s a rather suboptimal performance when also accounting for cross-country risk exposure undertaken.

Also, it would have been another thing if the said performance had been merely the result of stock market fluctuations without much regard to underlying fundamentals. However, there have been some very important developments underway since that article, which I will address in the present analysis, after which an investor might better be able to further evaluate whether to remain allocated to this ETF if they already have a position in it. For a fresh proposition, I would still consider it an avoid without any reservation.

Fund Overview

NFTY is a passive ETF that tracks the performance of an equity index called the NIFTY 50 Equal Weight Index. The said index is an equally weighted index that consists of the same companies as the NIFTY 50 Index, which tracks the performance of the 50 largest and most liquid Indian securities. While the NIFTY 50 is a free-float market capitalization-weighted index, NFTY is an equally weighted index. The security selection methodology is therefore relatively simple, allocating equally to the constituents of the flagship Nifty 50 index.

NFTY has not performed well for investors, significantly lagging the S&P 500 since the inception period. The fund was launched in February 2012. A meaningful portion of this underperformance is also attributable to currency drag resulting from persistent depreciation of the Indian INR versus the U.S. dollar, which tends to decrease total returns for a U.S.-based investor. That means that even if the underlying index is performing well, the total return reflected in NFTY would still lag. In addition, the fund charges an expense ratio of ~0.81%, which further increases the performance drag versus the underlying index. This makes it a peculiar case for an outside investor who may invest based on underlying domestic fundamentals but miss out on an important factor that meaningfully impacts overall returns.

The more important aspect, however, at present is not the prior performance but the recent developments underway. These evolving factors are likely to have a more direct bearing on forward expectations at present.

Recent Developments and Price Shocks in Energy Markets

The energy markets started witnessing excessive volatility from early March when Iran confirmed the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. That development came as quite unexpected for the markets. Brent is currently trading at around ~$115-120 levels. At the start of the calendar year, it was at around ~$60 levels, indicating a nearly 100% swing.

This reflects the extent of price volatility as a result of geopolitical uncertainty. India is the third-largest consumer of oil globally and imports over ~85% of its energy requirements. The increase in oil prices significantly impacts the current account deficit, the fiscal spending, the overall GDP, and inflation for the country. And the impact is negative in all of the cases. The below graph, although tentative in nature, reflects the impact of rising oil prices on the Indian economy.

The reason for emphasizing this aspect in a more pronounced manner is that unless there is any expectation whatsoever of energy prices moderating, I don’t see any case for an index fund allocation to India by a U.S. investor. An investment proposition may still be formed by a domestic investor investing in Indian equities, but when also accounting for currency-related depreciation, it becomes hard to justify an allocation by a U.S. investor.

The multiplicative impact of this is that not only does elevated energy prices reduce the investment merit even for a domestic investor on a standalone basis, who doesn’t need to worry about currency depreciation in the same manner as a U.S.-based investor, but the allocation becomes far more unattractive for a U.S.-based investor. Consider it this way: rising energy prices increase import bills, widening the current deficit. That directly impacts the overall economy and puts pressure on equities. The increase in energy prices at the same time also leads to more demand for dollars for procuring energy, which accelerates pressure on domestic currency. The depreciation of the currency thereby exerts additional pressure on equities and also capital outflows by foreign investors. While the relationship is not entirely linear and often has diverging influences, the core point remains intact of energy directly impacting both the underlying economy and capital markets trajectories. Simply stated, an environment that remains not constructive even for domestic investors becomes far more suboptimal for an outside investor. In addition, the majority of index funds allocate to similar equities and are essentially harvesting factor allocation rather than active security alpha and thereby being impacted by these factors primarily at large.

Currency Drag Persists

At the time of my initial coverage on NFTY, I highlighted structural demerits of the allocation with reference to currency depreciation and suggested caution. Now the same deficiencies stand further elevated to a meaningful extent. The long-term return depreciation of the Indian INR versus the USD over a 20-year period stands at a CAGR of ~3.8% now.

The INR, however, over the last two months has already depreciated by 3.46% versus the U.S. dollar. Leaving aside India’s IT sector, this, in addition to reducing total returns, also negatively impacts the majority of Indian industries. With reference to IT, they are at the same time facing another significant headwind from AI-related product launches and technological developments. In short, currency drag remains a critical factor underpinning the investment allocation to Indian broad-based equities by a foreign investor. And as highlighted above, the elevation of energy prices would likely continue to exert meaningful pressure on currencies. To highlight the extent of this, the RBI (India's central bank) had to recently direct banks to unwind INR positions in offshore derivative (NDF) markets to help support the INR.

Additional Comments on Market Valuations

An argument could, however, be made about the cyclicality of these developments and that they may be transitional. I don’t think that’s entirely the case at present. The Indian economy operates at a relatively higher base than it did twenty years prior. The expectation of similar historical returns, which occurred when the economy was in an accelerating phase, is not entirely reasonable. While the case for earnings expansion and formalization of the economy continues, a lot of this is already embedded in current market multiples. Also, the Buffett indicator in the Indian context (market cap to GDP) still suggests elevated valuations for broad-based Indian equities. That does not, by itself, reflect a lack of relative appeal, since the S&P is also suggestive of elevated valuations on that metric. However, when considering cross-border allocation along with currency headwinds and drag, that further discourages the investment case for NFTY.

A Few Positive Aspects Along With a Near-Term Possibility of a Rebound

Before concluding, however, I would also highlight a few aspects of the current portfolio which warrant discussion. In previous coverage, I had highlighted the inherent value tilt in an equal-weighted index and the element of mean reversion, which makes this allocation seemingly better than flagship market capitalization-weighted indices. Leaving aside the capital constraints of the strategy, this allocation on a relative basis is still suggestive of certain merits. For example, the underlying index that NFTY tracks has outperformed India’s flagship market capitalization-weighted Nifty 50 over the twenty-year period. The below data from NSE Indices reflects the same from 1999 to 2021.

In addition, if we see any moderation in energy prices resulting from easing geopolitical uncertainty, Indian markets could witness a sharp rebound. Foreign investors who have withdrawn money quickly could change their stance and begin redeploying capital to India as economic dynamics evolve, which may result in sharp appreciation in Indian equities from current prices. Also, the factors impacting the economy can change their relationships as situational dynamics evolve, and similar cause-and-effect relationships may not hold consistently, making market expectations difficult to set. The main focus, however, is that allocating currently to Indian equity-based funds such as NFTY is inherently risky, while a possible sharp rebound cannot be altogether ruled out.

In Conclusion

When I last covered NFTY, I suggested caution for this ETF, particularly for a U.S.-based investor. Since then, the investment proposition, if any, has weakened further. The energy shock, which led oil to exceed $100 levels, has resulted in the Indian economy and equities witnessing greater uncertainty, along with accelerating currency-related drag. Therefore, the hold rating continues to be justified. However, if we see any significant correction in Indian equities from these levels, a revisit might be needed. In addition, any conclusive evidence of structural moderation in the macro environment, especially in energy markets, might also warrant a revisit. With respect to current dynamics, this better remain being kept aside for the time being.

data.title: GOAU: Portfolio Companies Should Do Well, But Oil Prices Might Weigh On Near-Term Performance
data.description:
The U.S. Global GO GOLD and Precious Metal Miners ETF (GOAU) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund that can be used as a way of obtaining exposure to companies that either mine for precious metals or receive royalties and precious metal-denominated payments from gold mining projects. Both types of companies have performed phenomenally well over the past year, but they still appear to be very undervalued. Thus, a good argument could be made for obtaining exposure to these companies, which we will see in this article. The U.S. Global GO GOLD and Precious Metals ETF, unfortunately, might not be the best choice for investors who are in search of a high yield from their assets, as it only has a trailing twelve-month yield of 0.94% at the current price. This is lower than the S&P 500 Index (SP500) delivers, and as we all know, the current yield of the S&P 500 is also quite low. The fund does make it for this through its historical performance though, as this is one of the few funds that has managed to outperform the S&P 500 Index over the past ten years, and there is still reason to believe that it could deliver further upside.

About the U.S. Global GO GOLD And Precious Metal Miners ETF

The website for the U.S. Global GO GOLD and Precious Metal Miners ETF describes the fund thusly:

The U.S. Global GO GOLD and Precious Metal Miners ETF provides investors access to companies engaged in the production of precious metals either through active (mining or production) or passive (owning royalties or production streams) means.

This does not, admittedly, provide much insight into the fund other than to tell potential investors that the fund invests in companies that are involved in the precious metals industry. That could be a fairly expansive definition. It obviously includes companies such as Newmont (NEM), Agnico Eagle (AEM), and Barrick Mining (B) that primarily operate gold mines and have the bulk of their revenue come from the sale of precious metals. However, there are other mining companies that do not mine for gold as their primary product but do have sizable gold reserves or production. For example, BHP’s (BHP) Olympic Dam operation is actually the second-largest gold mining project in the world. That particular project holds approximately 65.60 million ounces of gold, which would be valued at $298.480 billion using today’s gold price of approximately $4,550 per ounce. BHP is not primarily a gold miner, as the company is primarily known for mining copper, iron ore, coal, and nickel. However, the current value of the gold in the company’s Olympic Dam project is more than double the current market capitalization of Newmont, so one could certainly argue that BHP qualifies as a mining company with exposure to precious metals production.

The prospectus for GOAU provides a better description of the fund’s strategy, as well as offering a definition of what the fund’s management considers to be a precious metals mining company. From the prospectus:

The Fund is an actively managed exchange-traded fund that seeks to achieve its investment objective by investing in exchange-listed common stock of U.S. and international companies that earn at least 50 percent of their aggregate revenue from precious metals. Precious metals consist of gold, silver, platinum, and palladium.

This tells us two important things about what the fund’s portfolio will contain. First, the fund only invests in common equity securities. We will not find bonds or preferred stock in the fund. Second, the fund’s portfolio will only include those companies that have precious metals as their primary product. A company such as Newmont or Agnico Eagle would qualify, whereas a company such as BHP would not, due to the fact that BHP is mostly mining for things other than gold despite its substantial gold reserves.

Later on in the prospectus, we see something else that is interesting and perhaps appealing, depending on your goals. The prospectus states that 80% of the fund’s assets will be invested in companies that have at least half of their revenues coming from gold, silver, platinum, palladium, or some combination of the four. The remaining 20% of its assets will be invested in the common equities of companies that are specifically focused on gold. Thus, the U.S. Global GO GOLD and Precious Metals Miners ETF is a precious metals mining fund, but we can expect it to have some exposure to gold miners at all times (even when the fundamentals might favor companies mining other precious metals). Gold is the only one of the four precious metals that is actively being purchased by central banks around the world, which provides a very wealthy and price-insensitive customer for gold producers that the other precious metals lack. As such, it might make sense to keep at least some exposure to gold miners specifically at all times, and this fund aims to do that.

Many of the fund’s largest holdings will be familiar to anyone who has followed the gold mining industry for any length of time. Here are the largest positions as of April 23, 2026:

We see a mixture of mining, royalty, and streaming companies here. Royal Gold (RGLD) and Franco-Nevada (FNV), for example, are both streaming companies that have interests in precious metals projects. A streaming company does not produce any metals itself, but it still has a lot of exposure to them, and it sees its revenues increase whenever precious metals prices do. Selborne Research offers the following description of a streaming company:

Royalty and streaming companies own cash flows from mining operations without bearing development, capital or operational risk. They fund miners in exchange for contractual rights to portions of production or revenue.

A precious metals streaming company basically makes loans to mining companies that need financing to develop a mine. Historically, mining companies have had difficulty getting banks to finance loans. This is particularly true for junior miners, some of which may need the financing to develop their first project and do not even have any cash flow. BDO Global, a major accounting and consulting firm, states that junior miners have been experiencing difficulty getting loans due to “conservative investor sentiment and monetary tightening.” While these may be valid reasons, another one is that mining projects frequently have uncertain cash flows. The amount of revenue that a mine earns is highly dependent on the prices of the commodities that are produced in that mine, and as we all know, commodity prices can be quite volatile. Furthermore, there is always the risk that the initial resource estimates for the mine were incorrect, which could cause the cash flows produced by the mine to be different (and potentially lower) than was originally projected. These factors could make banks, which are generally conservative lenders, to be hesitant to provide financing to a company that has found a promising site for a mine and wishes to develop it. Streaming companies came about as a solution to this problem. A streaming company provides financing to a mining company to develop a particular mine. In exchange, the streaming company receives the right to purchase a specific quantity of resources from the mine at a specified price.

A streaming company can earn very high profits from its financing deals in certain circumstances. This can be easily illustrated with an example. Let us assume that a streaming company (such as Royal Gold or Franco-Nevada) agreed to finance a gold mine in exchange for the right to purchase 20% of the gold produced by a mine at a price of $2,000 per ounce. The current price of gold is $4,550 per ounce or so, which means that the streaming company could purchase the gold for $2,000 per ounce and immediately turn around and sell it for $4,550 per ounce. That is a $2,550 per ounce profit, which is an incredibly high profit margin, and the only thing that the streaming company had to do in order to get this profit is to extend a loan to a mining company. If a project is successful and commodity prices are high, then these companies make a lot of money. For example, for the full-year 2025 period, Royal Gold had a gross profit of $890.9 million and an operating cash flow of $704.8 million on revenue of $1.0219 billion. That is an 87.18% gross profit margin and a 68.97% operating cash flow margin. Furthermore, because Royal Gold is able to purchase gold at a fixed price, its margins and profits increase whenever the price of gold increases. Streaming companies are frequently considered to be a low-risk way for investors to get exposure to precious metals prices, and I do not disagree with this statement. Royal Gold and Franco-Nevada are two of the largest companies in the precious metals streaming industry, and it is easy to see why GOAU has them as its two largest positions.

OR Royalties (OR) works a little bit differently. The company’s annual report provides the following description of the company:

OR Royalties is engaged in the business of acquiring and managing royalties, streams, and similar interests on precious metals and other commodities that fit the Company’s risk/reward objectives. The Company owns a portfolio of royalties, streams, options on royalty/stream financings and exclusive rights to participate in future royalty/stream financing on various projects.

OR Royalties invests in precious metals mining projects, and in exchange, it receives royalties and streaming rights to that particular project. We already discussed how streaming rights work, and royalty rights are similar, but not identical. A royalty right basically means that OR Royalties receives a portion of the revenue generated by the project over its remaining operational life. For example, OR Royalties has a 3%-5% net smelter return royalty on the Canadian Malartic Complex in Quebec, Canada. This means that OR Royalties receives 3%-5% of the net revenue of this mining project, with net revenue equal to (metal price times ounces produced) minus smelting, refining, and transport costs. This royalty is paid in fiat currency. As with streaming companies, this business model also results in substantial profit margins whenever resource prices are high. For the full-year 2025 period, OR Royalties had gross profit of $268.3 million and operating cash flow of $245.6 million, both on revenue of $277.4 million. This works out to a gross profit margin of 96.72% and an operating cash flow margin of 88.54%. Clearly, we can see that both royalty companies and streaming companies are able to achieve very high profit margins.

Other than Royal Gold, Franco-Nevada, and OR Royalties, all of the ten largest positions in GOAU’s portfolio are precious metals mining companies. These companies tend to have lower margins than streaming or royalty companies due to the simple fact that it is not cheap to construct or operate a mine. We can see this by looking at the financial figures for DPM Metals (DPM), which is the largest position in the portfolio of the U.S. Global GO GOLD and Precious Miners ETF apart from the two streamers and OR Royalties. Here are that company’s revenues and gross profits over the past nine years:

(all figures in millions of Canadian dollars)

For the full-year 2025 period, DPM Metals had total revenue of C$1.3046 billion and gross profits of C$831.7 million. That is only a 63.75% gross profit margin, which is clearly lower than the streamers possessed (although it is still very good compared to many other companies in the market).

One thing that we immediately notice about DPM Metals’ financial figures is that its gross profit margins have been improving over time. This is shown in the following chart:

(all figures in millions of Canadian dollars, except for percentages)

As shown, DPM Metals saw its gross margins increase at a rather remarkable rate over the past nine years. The biggest reason for this is that the price of gold, silver, and other precious metals has been rising over the period. This chart shows the spot price of gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and silver (XAGUSD:CUR) over the period from December 31, 2016, through December 31, 2025:

In this chart, gold is represented by the black line, and silver is represented by the blue line. As is clearly shown, both metals saw their prices more than double (silver more than tripled) over the period. At its core, a precious metals mining company extracts gold and silver from the ground and sells them in the market. The amount of revenue that the mining company earns is a direct function of the resources, so whenever precious metals prices increase as much as they have over this period, we can expect it to cause the revenue of a precious metals mining company to increase, all else being equal. The costs of running mines did not increase nearly as quickly as precious metals prices, which we can see in the above chart that clearly shows that DPM Metals’ cost of revenues was pretty stable over the nine-year period. The combination of these two factors resulted in DPM Metals’ gross profits rising over time.

We can see the same trend of rising margins if we look at Coeur Mining (CDE), which is the fifth-largest position in the fund’s portfolio, and the second-largest position that is actually a mining company (rather than a streamer or a royalty company). Here are the company’s financial figures over the past nine years:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

This chart shows Coeur Mining’s gross margins over the same period:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars, except for the percentages)

Admittedly, Coeur Mining had a lot more volatility with respect to its gross margins than DPM Metals, but we can still see a similar trend of the company’s gross margins increasing (particularly in 2024 and 2025). The surge in gold and silver prices that has occurred over the past two years is the biggest reason for this.

Most gold miners do not have production costs that are anywhere near to the current price of gold. The usual metric used by the industry to evaluate production costs is something that is known as All-in Sustaining Cost. The World Gold Council developed this metric in 2013 to help investors and analysts understand and compare the costs that a mining company incurs to produce a single ounce of gold. Here is the formula:

While gold prices themselves are set globally, the costs of producing in some jurisdictions can be more expensive than in others. For example, labor costs are higher in the United States than in many South American nations, which has an impact on the mining company’s expenses. Most miners have all-in sustaining costs below $1,500 per ounce. Bunker Group provides the following chart showing the approximate all-in sustaining costs by region:

The global average to mine gold is about $1,400 to $1,450 per ounce. Some markets are a bit cheaper than others, however, with South America generally being under $1,250 per ounce. DPM Metals stated in its fourth-quarter earnings report that its all-in sustaining costs averaged $1,450 per gold equivalent ounce sold. That is well below today’s $4,550 per ounce gold price, so we can very quickly start to see that many of the companies in GOAU’s portfolio are highly profitable in today’s market environment.

As the revenues, cash flows, and profits of precious metals miners tend to increase with precious metals prices, we can draw the assumption that their stock prices will exhibit a correlation with precious metals prices. This is, in fact, the case, which we can see in this chart:

This chart shows the spot prices of gold (black line) and silver (blue line) over the past five years. I have added the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) and the U.S. Global GO GOLD and Precious Metal Miners ETF to the chart to show the comparison. As we can see, both of the exchange-traded funds delivered share price appreciation that exhibited a high correlation to the spot prices of gold and silver. Admittedly, it is not a perfect correlation, but it is a strong enough correlation that we can make the general statement that the share prices of the companies in the U.S. Global GO GOLD and Precious Metal Miners ETF should rise whenever gold and silver prices are rising. As GOAU is an exchange-traded fund, its share price is usually very close to its net asset value, so we should expect the fund’s share price to rise whenever gold prices are rising (and vice versa).

As such, we should take a look at the macroeconomic backdrop for gold and silver as part of our thesis for this fund.

Outlook For Gold And Silver

Gold, along with other precious metals, has been one of the best-performing asset classes over the past year. As shown here, the spot price of gold has risen by 36.62% over the past year, outperforming the S&P 500 Index (SP500):

However, gold’s track record of strong performance against the S&P 500 Index dates back well before last year. Over the period from 1961 to 1968, the central banks of the Western developed nations (United States, United Kingdom, Germany, France, Italy, Belgium, Netherlands, and Switzerland) coordinated their efforts to keep the price of gold at $35 per ounce. This was known as the London Gold Pool. On March 15, 1968, the London Gold Pool temporarily shut down as declining global confidence in the U.S. dollar led to such a strong increase in the demand for gold that the central banks were no longer able to maintain the gold peg. The London Gold Pool formally closed down on March 29, 1968. At the time, the price of gold was at $35.20 per ounce. The S&P 500 Index closed at $90.20 on the same day. Since that date, the price of gold has risen by 12,826.14% to today’s price of $4,550 per ounce. The S&P 500 Index is only up by 7,791.13% over the same period. It is true that an investor who reinvested all of the dividends paid out by every company in the S&P 500 Index since March 1968 would have done better than if they had held gold, but in terms of pure price appreciation, gold has outperformed large-capitalization stocks over the past 58 years.

At this point, there might be some investors who point out that gold’s performance so far in 2026 has been much weaker than the yellow metal delivered in 2025. In January, the price of gold reached an all-time high of $5,557.83 per ounce before declining to today’s level of $4,550 per ounce. However, gold has still beaten the S&P 500 Index year-to-date (albeit barely):

We can see that gold has been struggling since late February, and the biggest reason for that is the Iran War. This may seem counterintuitive due to the fact that gold is frequently considered to be a hedge against fear and uncertainty. As Fire Capital Management points out:

In times of economic uncertainty, gold has historically proven to be a resilient asset, outperforming traditional equities and providing stability to client portfolios.

We would logically think that gold should do well with the outbreak of war in the Middle East, as investors would be expected to purchase it as a safe haven trade. However, the key point that this assumption overlooks is the fact that crude oil is priced in U.S. dollars. Oil prices have risen substantially due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and the resulting supply shortage of crude oil, which I explained in a recent article. The increase in oil prices has forced countries around the world to increase the U.S. dollars that they keep on hand in order to pay for their oil imports. The way that they obtain more U.S. dollars is by purchasing them in the foreign exchange market. This puts buying pressure on the U.S. dollar and causes it to strengthen against other assets, including gold. If we look at gold priced in euros, it is up 6.15% year-to-date:

Gold is up 7.98% year-to-date priced in Japanese yen:

Gold is up 5.72% year-to-date priced in Swiss francs, and gold is up 5.66% priced in British pounds. In other words, the outbreak of war in Iran has not caused gold weakness so much as it has caused the U.S. dollar to strengthen. Even measured in U.S. dollars, gold has still beaten large-capitalization American stocks year-to-date, so the general historical perception that gold is a safe-haven asset that benefits from uncertainty appears to remain intact. In this case, though, the U.S. dollar is also benefiting from safe-haven status, and many countries believe that they need oil more than they need gold, and in order to get oil, they need to have enough U.S. dollars on hand to purchase it. Thus, the decline in the price of gold over the past two months since the Iran War began has not been a story of gold weakness as much as it has been a story of rising oil prices and U.S. dollar strength.

I strongly suspect that the price of gold will rise over the long-term. One of the biggest reasons for this is that the U.S. fiscal deficit will likely pressure the Federal Reserve to step in and begin monetizing the federal debt issuances. The Federal Reserve has already started to do this on a fairly small scale, as it has been purchasing $40 billion worth of U.S. Treasury securities every month since December 2025. This is increasing the size of the Federal Reserve’s balance sheet, and it has contributed to the increase in the M2 money supply over the past few months. The M2 money supply has increased by 1.49% since December:

Please note that this chart only shows the M2 money supply through March. It is reasonable to assume that it has increased further in April, especially when we consider that the M2 money supply has increased in every single month over the past year. Based on the increase in M2 during the first three months of the year, the money supply is increasing at approximately a 5.95% annualized rate. This is far more than the projected rate of change in the gross domestic product of the United States, which represents an inflationary scenario. After all, economics tells us that when the money supply increases faster than the growth in real output, we get inflation.

Historically, gold has done a pretty good job of maintaining its value in real terms in the face of inflation. Back in 1920, the average house in the United States cost around $6,296. The price of gold in 1920 was $20.67 per ounce, so the average house was about 305 ounces of gold. Today, 305 ounces of gold is worth about $1.388 million. In most areas of the United States, $1.388 million will buy a pretty nice house. Elemetal recently did a study in which the researchers attempted to determine how many one-kilogram gold bars it would take to purchase the median house in the United States. While it has varied over time, the relationship between the two shows much more stability than if we were to price houses in U.S. dollars:

We can see that, during most years, the average house could be purchased with fewer than ten kilograms of gold. The sole exception was during the 1950s and 1960s, which also happened to be the period of time in which central banks were attempting to maintain an artificial peg of the currency to gold (see the London Gold Pool discussion above). Over the same period (1890 to 2025), the average price of a house measured in U.S. dollars went from $3,557 to $326,565. Clearly, gold did a much better job of holding its value against houses over time than the U.S. dollar did.

The takeaway here is that the money supply is growing faster than the actual economic production of the United States. That is quite likely to cause inflation, and gold has historically maintained its value in the face of inflation. Thus, it seems likely that gold prices will rise over the long term, and this should have a beneficial effect on the revenues and profits of the streaming, royalty, and mining companies held in the portfolio of the U.S. Global GO GOLD and Precious Metal Miners ETF. The share prices of those companies should rise as a result, and this should provide a tailwind for the shares of the ETF.

Expenses

The U.S. Global GO GOLD and Precious Metal Miners ETF has an expense ratio of 0.60%. This is not too bad for an actively managed fund, as the passively managed VanEck Gold Miners ETF has an expense ratio of 0.51%, which is not really that much lower than GOAU’s expense ratio. The Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF (GBUG), which is another actively managed gold mining ETF, has a net expense ratio of 0.89% (higher than GOAU).

Distributions

The U.S. Global GO GOLD and Precious Metals Miners ETF does not employ any strategies that are intended to provide investors with increased yields. For example, the fund does not use option strategies or leverage, which are two of the more popular methods used by some of the more income-oriented funds. All that this fund does is receive the dividends paid out by the companies that it holds and then distributes them to its own shareholders after deducting its expenses. This has resulted in this fund only having a 0.94% yield. This is lower than the 1.04% yield of the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust, but it is slightly higher than the 0.72% yield of the VanEck Gold Miners ETF.

Once again, this fund is not intended as an income investment as there is no guarantee that the distribution yield will be sustainable. The fund simply pays out the dividends that it receives from the common equities in its portfolio after deducting its expenses. While I suspect that the companies in the portfolio will do fairly well going forward as the price of gold recovers (and most of them have production costs that are far lower than the current price of gold), the fact that this fund can change its holdings at any time means that its income from the portfolio companies will vary, and by extension, so will its distribution.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the U.S. Global GO GOLD and Precious Miners ETF is an actively managed exchange-traded fund that invests in companies that are involved in the precious metals industry. In order to be included in the fund, a company must derive at least 50% of its revenue from gold or other precious metals. Over the past two years, the price of all precious metals has increased significantly, and this has allowed these companies to earn very attractive profits as their costs are much lower than today’s gold price. I strongly believe that gold and other precious metals will rise in price over the long term, as the money supply is rising faster than the productive output of the U.S. economy. Many other developed countries are in a similar position, as the International Monetary Fund pointed out two weeks ago. This positions the companies held in GOAU’s portfolio for strong performance going forward, although high oil prices and a strong U.S. dollar may be a drag for the next few months.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Meta's Earnings Paradox: A Brilliant Quarter The Market Chose To Punish
data.description:
What?

Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) delivered a very strong financial performance in its latest earnings report. The company beat most of the key metrics analysts were watching. Revenue came in at $56.31 billion, ahead of consensus. Adjusted EPS was $7.31, above the $6.78 estimate. The Family of Apps segment, which includes Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp and Threads, generated $26.9 billion in operating income. A major factor contributing to this is the growth in advertising revenue, and the fact that advertisers were willing to pay approximately 12 percent more per advertisement as compared to prior year.

However, despite delivering what many would describe as a very strong quarter of performance, shares of Meta Platforms declined more than 6% immediately after hours.

In essence, a single revision triggered an immediate reassessment of Meta's investment thesis. Prior to the guidance update, many investors were very comfortable with the concept of Meta as a company that generates high margins and has relatively low capital expenditures related to its advertising business. However, investors now face a new reality: Meta is likely to become one of the most capital-intensive companies in the tech sector given the rapid growth in its investments into AI Infrastructure.

Whether the market’s reaction is warranted or if they are missing something much larger will be debated. Meta is investing in its core business because it believes the core business is so successful that it can afford to invest in AI infrastructure which could ultimately lead to the creation of one of the most valuable AI monetization engines in the world.

No longer is the debate centered around whether Meta can generate cash flow. Instead, the attention has moved toward whether the markets views investments in AI CapEx as a cost issue or rather as the investment associated with creating a competitive moat that would make it impossible for smaller companies to compete against Meta.

What Meta Actually Reported

As shown on the dedicated page on Seeking Alpha, the results in Meta’s first quarter were clearly very good. Total revenue reached $56.31 billion (vs. about $55.45 billion) and the company beat the $6.78 consensus for net adjusted EPS ($7.31). As we know, Adjusted EPS is the metric that best reflects the company's performance.

Additionally, Meta reported $10.44 GAAP diluted EPS, that’s $2.13 more than the adjusted EPS. The reason for that large discrepancy is an $8.03 billion income tax benefit that was included in the Q1 GAAP diluted EPS calculation.

Operating Income totaled $22.87B, increasing by 30% YoY, and the operating margin was approximately 40.6%. Additionally, free cash flow for the quarter totaled $12.39B, although the company did deploy $19.0B in capital expenditures during that same time frame. In other words, Meta is still generating significant cash, even while funding one of the largest infrastructure buildouts in corporate history.

In terms of revenue, the Family of Apps segment, Meta’s core advertising platform, generated $55.91 billion dollars (versus the estimate of $55 billion), and operating income of $26.90 billion (versus the estimate of $24.26 billion). Advertising revenue alone totaled $55.02 billion (versus estimates of $54.16 billion), and perhaps most importantly, the average price-per-ad rose 12% compared to last year, indicating that Meta’s use of AI to improve ad targeting and relevance is allowing the company to earn more from every impression it sells.

Revenue from Reality Labs, Meta’s virtual & augmented reality group, totaled $402M (missing the estimate of $508M), however, the operating loss narrowed to $4.03B versus the projected $4.77B. Although Reality Labs still weighs heavily on profitability, those losses continue to narrow vs expectations and represent less than 1% of Meta’s overall revenue.

Why the Market Reacted Negatively Despite the Beat

There were no negative financials, there were excellent financials. The reason for the decline was because of changes to the estimate of the amount of money spent by Meta on capital expenditures. Meta now estimates it will spend somewhere between $125 billion and $145 billion on capital expenditures (CAPEX) in 2026. That is an increase over the prior estimated range of $115 billion to $135 billion. The increased spending reflects both increased prices for components used in building new equipment and increased costs associated with adding new data centers, said the earnings release from Meta.

Meta investors have a number of questions. One question is simply: how can they put this huge number into context? The $145 billion Meta is now estimating for capex in one year is larger than many of the revenues generated by other Fortune 500 companies each year.

Another issue is that the revised estimate indicates that investors may not be able to rely as heavily as thought upon their expectations for predictability of expenses for artificial intelligence-related hardware. If Meta is raising their forecast today, then what keeps them from revising again next quarter?

Perhaps more important is the problem of linking the capex dollars to incremental revenue growth. While Meta’s advertising business continues to perform well, investors have difficulty determining how much of that success is driven by investments in AI vs. the natural trend of users toward using more social media.

Additionally, there is a structural problem. Historically, Meta has been viewed by the investing public as a high-margin, asset-light platform business. It was the ability of Meta to earn large amounts of profit without needing to invest significant amounts of capital that drove the attractiveness of investing in the company. However, as capital expenditures rise at Meta, it begins to appear more like a capital intensive infrastructure-type business. Therefore, if the investing public views Meta as less asset-light and more like an infrastructure type business, the stock price may experience some degree of permanent multiple contraction regardless of continued increases in profitability.

Adding to investor unease, The Wall Street Journal reported that Meta is experiencing a “significant” server supply deficit. As such, while Meta plans to spend $145 billion in capex, there appears to be the potential for ongoing escalation in spending above current estimates.

The Bullish Case

While we understand why investors have focused on capex spending, this is potentially overshadowing the strength shown in Meta’s underlying business. As indicated by Q1, Meta’s core advertising business is not only strong, but it continues to accelerate. In fact, Meta’s advertising revenues showed a substantial increase and the 12 percent year over year growth in average ad price is very telling. This type of growth indicates that Meta isn’t just generating more ads; it is also creating better ads. Advertisers are ready to invest extra for ads that work better for them.

In many ways this is where AI becomes real. Meta has invested heavily in machine-learning-based ad targeting systems and predictive modeling tools that improve creative delivery. All of these advancements enable advertisers to get much better ROI on their ad spend. With better ROI, Meta can charge more for its ads. The 12% year-over-year increase in average price per ad is consistent with the idea that Meta’s AI-driven improvements in targeting and relevance are already supporting better monetization.

Meta’s Family of Apps business unit produced $26.9 billion in operating income during Q1. This beat analyst expectations by more than $2.5 billion. This isn’t a business under stress, it’s a business producing at an extremely high level of scale and profit margins. Meta’s consolidated operating margin was approximately 40.6%. The Family of Apps business was even stronger, generating about $26.90 billion of operating income on $55.91 billion of revenue, implying an operating margin of roughly 48%. To put that number into perspective; this is among the top few companies in tech when it comes to converting customer engagement into cash.

Meta produced $12.39 billion in free cash flow alone in Q1. After putting $19.0 billion toward capex expenses, Meta had plenty of cash left over. Annualizing Q1 free cash flow would imply roughly $50 billion of annual free cash flow. But investors should be careful with that simple run-rate, because Meta’s full-year capex guidance implies much higher spending over the rest of the year. There is certainly enough free cash flow to invest in AI, distribute some back to shareholders, and keep some cash available for emergencies.

Many people view Reality Labs as a major issue regarding Meta’s viability as a company. The reality labs segment lost less than expected money during Q1. While Reality Labs continues to lose money, the trend is positive, and the amount of money being spent here compared to what is being generated from the family of apps business unit is reasonable. Meta is able to take big bets on virtual/augmented reality now, because its main line of business is so profitable.

Most importantly, Meta is using its own strength to invest in AI, rather than trying to raise capex funding due to declining ad sales. That makes a huge difference. Instead of needing to worry about whether or not they have enough money to build one of the largest AI infrastructure projects ever undertaken, Meta can afford to fund it from the profits it generates from advertising and other areas of its family of apps business units. If Meta can absorb this level of capex while still producing substantial free cash flow, that would not indicate distress. It would show the underlying power of the business model. That shows a tremendous amount of power and control.

AI Capex May Be a Moat, Not a Red Flag

What if Meta is creating a structural advantage in the use of artificial intelligence (AI) in advertising and content recommendation? What if the cap-ex at meta is the cost of admission into a whole new competitive landscape?

In the case of AI-driven advertising, all interactions by users of Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, Messenger, Reels and Threads create data that can be used to train machine learning models. With each additional user interaction, meta gets a larger dataset. Each additional dataset makes the machine learning model(s) at meta get better and each improved model gives meta greater ad targeting capabilities. With more effective ad targeting, meta sells more ads inventory and can therefore command higher prices for those ads.

The result is a flywheel effect that is nearly impossible for competitors to replicate. Small advertising platforms lack the scale of user data that meta has. They lack the compute budget that meta has. They lack the distribution channels that meta has. Most importantly, small platforms do not have the capability to monetize incremental improvements in ad targeting on multiple surfaces simultaneously. Meta can take one improvement to its AI model and apply it across Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp at once to billions of users. Small platforms can’t.

The 12% increase in average price per ad is already signaling how well this fly wheel is working. Meta is not just selling more ads. It is selling more expensive ads. If AI improves the efficiency of ad conversion rates going forward, then we would expect pricing power for meta to continue to build. This isn't a margin compression story. This is a monetization improvement story due to technological advantage.

Also, I think the market under values the strategic dimension of this spend. While some might say that meta is just building AI infrastructure for advertising, others argue that meta is building a broad base of AI enabled products/services such as business messaging tools, creator monetization tools, etc. As mentioned above, CEO Mark Zuckerberg said the company had “a milestone quarter with strong momentum across our apps and the release of our first model from Meta Superintelligence Labs.” This implies to me that meta is positioning itself to compete in both advertising and the broader AI product landscape.

Therefore, if meta can successfully monetize AI across multiple product categories (advertising, business messaging, creators’ monetization and consumer AI assistants), then capex spend may turn out to be extremely prescient in retrospect.

Real Risks and The Negative Side of Things

Clearly, if on the one hand it appears to be a positive factor, it is clear that on the other hand it can represent a negative factor. Their capex forecast upper bound increased from $135 billion to $145 billion. There is nothing to stop this from happening again, especially if component pricing continues to increase or if Meta runs into more capacity issues. As capex continues to grow, free cash flow can shrink. A shrinking free cash flow means less money available to return value back to shareholders in the form of share repurchases and/or dividend payments. This could negatively affect the stock, especially once investors see Meta as a low quality cash generating machine.

Also, there is always execution risk. Meta is betting on AI-based infrastructures being able to deliver better targeted advertising (and therefore improved ad prices), higher user engagement and new sources of revenue. There is no way to know whether Meta will realize the ROI they expect when investing over $145 billion into AI. Training and deploying large-scale AI models are expensive.

Reality Labs has continued to represent a huge drain on Meta’s profitability. Reality Labs lost $4.03 billion in just one quarter, which was better than expected. However, it represented a huge burn of cash. Meta believes that VR/AR will eventually be major platforms. But we don’t know how long that will take. Investors generally have zero tolerance for long term bets that don’t produce near term results.

Another area of risk for Meta is regulation. Meta conducts business in a highly regulated industry and regulators across major jurisdictions continue to scrutinize Meta’s data collection practices, content moderation policies and competitive behavior, content moderation policies and competitive behaviors. Any negative regulatory actions against Meta could prohibit it from collecting data, targeting advertisements or operating its platforms as they are today.

Lastly, there is the possibility of valuing Meta down due to multiple compression. If investors begin viewing Meta as a capital intensive infrastructure company rather than as an asset light platform, then they will continually compress the multiples paid for the stock. Today, Meta is trading at a forward P/E of approximately 22x. That is lower than all of the other mega-cap companies within technology. But if investors lose faith in Meta’s ability to earn high returns on their AI capex, then those multiples will go even lower.

Valuation and Investment Implications

Meta’s consensus estimates indicate that diluted Earnings Per Share ("EPS") will increase by 16% compounded annually over the next 2 years from $33.27 in 2026 to $47.92 in 2028.

This represents a very favorable earnings growth profile that, when compared to the Company's current multiple, results in a PEG ratio of 1.03, indicating that investors are paying a reasonable multiple for the Company's expected growth despite concerns regarding Meta's increasing capital expenditures.

My target price is $975, or about 46 percent higher than today. I believe it is reasonable given various ways to value the company using improved monetization trends and potential multiple expansions based on expected visibility of AI returns. If Meta can continue to operate within a 34 – 35% margin rate by 2027 (as projected) and show that the twelve percent annual increase in the average ad price per unit was an aberration and instead the start of increasing long-term pricing power, then the stock may be able to earn a forward P/E multiple of 22-23x.

The price would also align with the company’s past premium valuation and be closer to those of comparable companies such as Microsoft, which are trading at 25x. Using the 20.3x multiple, we obtain a price of $975, with EPS of $47.93 in 2028. This price will have been earned if Meta is able to provide evidence of measurable returns generated by AI capital expenditures in terms of stable ad prices, consistent margins, and additional revenue from AI enabled products.

My Conclusion

Meta’s Q1 results were not a bad quarter — they are an exceptionally good quarter derailed by one thing: the market simply doesn’t know what to put on an AI capital expenditure (capex). At least for now, there is no doubt about the quality of the underlying business. Revenue exceeded estimates, net earnings/adjusted EPS exceeded estimates, operating income was impressive and ad pricing improved. Additionally, operating income from the "Family of Apps" was a whopping $26.9B. So the issue isn't with the business itself; the issue is with the cost that Meta is paying to protect and grow that business.

Of course I get why investors would be concerned. Capex continues to rise and investors will continue to have difficulty isolating the portion of revenue growth attributable to AI. Those are legitimate concerns. However, I also believe the market may be viewing this through a far too narrow lens. Meta is not spending money out of weakness. Instead, it is spending money because of tremendous strength. Specifically, Meta has unparalleled reach through its various applications, and can apply AI-based enhancements across nearly every user on those platforms instantaneously. Few other companies have that ability. To me, therefore, the most important number reported in the last quarter is not just the capex guidance. Rather, it is the 12% increase in the average price per advertisement. This represents the first indicator that AI may potentially be improving Meta's ability to generate revenue from advertisements. While it is not yet clear whether this trend will continue or if it will ultimately lead to meaningful improvement in monetization (and thus profitability), it is precisely the type of indicator that investors need to monitor.

My opinion about what's happening with the results is simple. Meta is not spending money as you would traditionally define that. Meta is paying an extremely large amount of money for something much bigger than just making its ads better: It is trying to build an "advertising moat" which will be hard to compete against or copy, and one that is now being driven by artificial intelligence. That is risky, but it is also exactly the kind of risk I want a company to take when the core business is still this strong.

data.title: Adlai Nortye: Recapitalized, Refocused, But Still Too Early To Upgrade
data.description:
Adlai Nortye Ltd. (ANL) is a small biopharmaceutical company that develops preclinical/early-stage candidates in oncology. Specifically, they’re working on AN9025 for RAS-mutated solid tumors, AN4035 for solid tumors, and AN8025 for advanced tumors. ANL’s AN4005 is another small molecule for advanced tumors, and Palupiprant (AN0025) is being tested with chemoradiotherapy for rectal and esophageal cancers. Yet, I believe that most of its pipeline still needs more trial data to make me lean bullish or bearish on the stock itself. However, we do know that ANL’s recent PIPE raises have given it ample financing to pursue these candidates. Hence, for now, I feel it’s fair to remain neutral on the stock until we see more concrete trial data.

Recapitalized and Refocused Oncology Biotech

Adlai Nortye Ltd. is a clinical-stage biotechnology company that develops therapies targeting RAS-driven cancers and cancer immunotherapies. The company was incorporated back in 2018, and it went public in 2023. ANL has subsidiaries that operate in mainland China and the US. However, ANL’s key operating subsidiaries appear to be Adlai Nortye USA in the US, while its Chinese subsidiaries are Adlai Nortye Biopharma and Shanghai Adlai Nortye Biopharma. Overall, you can think of the ADSs as the Cayman holding company that has subsidiaries in a group structure. Interestingly, though, they recently raised a substantial amount of capital from several institutional investors, and its valuation seems reasonable, which is why I felt it was worthwhile looking into this stock.

As a quick overview, ANL’s bull case mostly hinged on Buparlisib (AN2025). At the time, it was in Phase 3 for head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC). So, ANL was basically a bet on Buparlisib's potential approval contingent on that registrational trial. Novartis originally developed Buparlisib, and they concluded that Buparlisib had a challenging safety profile. After that, ANL's Phase 3 BURAN trial for HNSCC also failed. So, ANL terminated the program, and that meant its near-term bull case was invalidated to a large extent. But since then, ANL has raised new funds (more on this later) and refocused its research towards other interesting oncology candidates.

With that in mind, ANL’s pipeline today still has several oncology candidates, including RAS-targeting therapies and next-generation antibodies. In the first category, ANL is studying AN9025, which is an oral pan-RAS(ON) inhibitor. This asset is in Phase 1 for RAS-mutated solid tumors. AN9025 is in Phase 1a clinical trials, and management is targeting to complete Phase 1 escalation by 1H2027. ANL holds rights to AN9025 outside China, while ASK Pharm holds rights in China, Hong Kong, and Macao.

For context, a pan-RAS(ON) inhibitor helps to block active RAS signaling across multiple mutations. RAS proteins act like molecular switches inside cells. When RAS is “ON,” it can activate pathways that promote cell growth and proliferation. In cancer, the RAS mutations can keep RAS in an active state that keeps the cells receiving growth signals even when they should not. Therefore, a pan-RAS(ON) tries to block active RAS signaling across several cancer indications. And, in theory, I think this sounds like a promising approach because it could eventually grow into a flexible asset with multiple indications. However, even though it’s ANL’s main strategic RAS asset, it’s still years away from any potential regulatory approvals.

Other Pipeline Assets

Aside from AN9025, ANL has AN4035 as a CEACAM5-targeting antibody-drug conjugate (ADC). ADC is the delivery system that helps guide the therapy itself toward cancer cells while carrying an attached payload of a pan-RAS(ON) that’s intended to kill those cells. However, even though it sounds promising on paper, this candidate is only in the IND-enabling stages. ANL intends to submit its IND for solid tumors by mid-2026. And it appears like a fully in-house asset, which is why they hold global rights for it as well.

I’ll also mention briefly other assets like AN8025 in immuno-oncology, which is a tri-specific antibody fusion protein targeting the immune checkpoints PD-L1 and LAG3. AN8025 is in Phase 1a for advanced tumors, with Phase 1 dose escalation completion expected by year-end 2026. Additionally, AN4005 is an oral small-molecule PD-L1 inhibitor studied in Phase 1b for advanced tumors, with a clinical update planned by 2H2026.

Lastly, Palupiprant (AN0025) is another interesting asset as a potential chemoradiotherapy enhancer. It’s technically an EP4 antagonist that blocks a pathway related to immune suppression and tumor progression in some cancers. ANL holds global rights for Palupiprant, except in Japan, Korea, Taiwan, and Southeast Asian countries. Currently, ANL is testing Palupiprant for two indications. The first one is neoadjuvant rectal cancer plus chemoradiotherapy (Neoadj RC (+CRT)). Neoadjuvant means treatments, such as chemotherapy and radiation therapy, given before the main treatment, usually before surgery.

This program is in Phase 2, which would make it its most advanced candidate. Its topline results are expected by 1H2027. ANL also estimates that by 2028, the market population will be 19,000 patients in the US and 50,000 patients across other major European markets and Japan. Palupiprant’s second indication is locally advanced esophageal cancer plus chemoradiotherapy (LA EC (+CRT)). ANL plans a clinical update by 2H2026 for this other indication. However, unlike AN9025, Palupiprant isn’t part of a licensing deal or collaboration, which is why I don’t think it’s their key asset right now. Plus, if you look at ANL’s pipeline chart above, they group Palupiprant among “Other Assets” themselves.

Valuation and Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, ANL currently trades at a $486.8 million market cap. Their April 2026 20-F report shows their balance sheet holds $8.1 million in cash against $24.6 million in borrowings. However, those figures were at the December 2025 cutoff, so they don’t reflect the recent raises they’ve done in 2026. By February 2026, ANL announced a $140 million PIPE. Then, they did another $150 million PIPE in April 2026. And I think we should also include the $8.7 million payment they received in January 2026 from ASK Pharm under their exclusive license agreement. That way, I estimate ANL’s “updated” cash balances are likely closer to $306.8 million.

Naturally, remember that ANL doesn’t have any product revenues at the moment. But based on its cash alone, it would suggest ANL trades at roughly 1.6x its “updated” cash balances, which I think seems reasonable for a newly recapitalized biotech. This is especially true if AN9025 or AN4035 can validate management’s bet on targeting RAS. However, this also points to ANL’s biggest risk, which is that it’s now a mostly preclinical/early-stage stock after Buparlisib’s Phase 3 was terminated.

Moreover, I calculate ANL burned through approximately $33.7 million during 2025. Note that I got that figure by simply adding its 2025 cash flows from operations and CAPEX. On paper, this would imply a very comfortable cash runway of 9.1 years. Having said that, I anticipate their cash burn will probably increase considerably once they start more advanced trials on AN9025, AN4035, AN8025, and AN4005. However, remember that ANL’s pipeline pivot places them far from any realistic near-term regulatory approvals.

As I previously highlighted, their main value is most likely AN9025. I say this for two reasons. First, this is actually part of a licensing deal with a mid-sized Chinese pharmaceutical. But also because ANL’s presentation frames AN9025 as having best-in-class potential within Pan-RAS(ON). Unfortunately, AN9025 is only in Phase 1, and its next major catalyst would be completing dose escalation by 1H2027. So, until then, I’d argue AN9025 still needs more data that supports that notion. Yet, in my experience, whenever management highlights that a candidate has best-in-class potential, it means they often consider it the main strategic focus as well.

Aside from its lead candidate, AN4035 is in even earlier stages with its IND submission by mid-2026. Similarly, AN8025 is also in Phase 1, and its dose escalation should be ready by the end of 2026. ANL also mentioned they’ll announce a clinical update for AN4005 during 2H2026. So realistically speaking, I wouldn’t expect ANL to have any regulatory approvals in the near term unless any of these earlier-stage candidates produce unusually positive data, which is inherently highly speculative.

Conclusion: Fresh Start and Still Speculative

Overall, ANL has had to refocus on other oncology assets after its previous flagship candidate, Buparlisib, was discontinued. Fortunately, it’s a good sign that they managed to secure sizeable PIPE raises from institutional investors that shored up its balance sheet. I think ANL today probably has enough cash to produce new clinical data on its other oncology bets over time. However, for now, I’d argue that all of its R&D programs are still too early-stage to make a bullish or bearish call. Similarly, I think the stock itself trades at a reasonable cash multiple, so I don’t see a major valuation disparity either. Hence, I feel it’s fair to deem ANL a “Hold” for now until they deliver fresh data on their candidates later this year.

data.title: The AI Bubble Is Starting To Crack
data.description:
Thesis Summary

While we can’t deny that headline data still supports the narrative of the AI supercycle, cracks are beginning to form in the industry.

OpenAI recently missed internal revenue targets, and AI faces many other challenges.

Markets are forward-looking, and one must wonder if the upside in AI is now fully priced in after a record rally in the semiconductors and other associated stocks.

If the bubble is ready to pop, so to speak, these are the warning signs investors should be on the lookout for.

Credit Markets: The First Crack Starts Here

While, yes, stocks are forward-looking, nothing is more forward-looking than credit markets.

The simplest and most straightforward way to tell if the market is in distress is by looking at credit spreads, or Credit Default Swaps.

A lot of analysts have been recently tracking the CDS of Oracle (ORCL) bonds, for example. The company has made some major investments into data centers, and despite having strong cash flows, it has sought financing.

We can see that five-year CDS for Oracle bonds reached a high in March, and though they have since cooled off, they have climbed back up in the last week.

So now more than ever, investors need to keep an eye on the debt markets. Stress in these markets tends to show up 6–12 months before equities react.

We’ve seen this movie before. Telecom credit, for example, deteriorated quietly in the late 1990s while equities kept rallying.

During the decade, companies like WorldCom, Global Crossing, and Qwest invested more than $500 billion in laying down fiber optic cable.

Like with AI, this seemed like a no-brainer. Internet usage was allegedly doubling every 100 days.

But everyone was fighting for the same pie, and eventually these investments became unprofitable, credit markets deteriorated, and telecom stocks lost $2 trillion in market value from 2000 to 2002

Employment: The Good And The Bad

Employment is usually a lagging indicator, but hiring isn’t.

Job postings have been steadily declining into 2026, and this will eventually become a problem.

Of course, there’s an idea that AI efficiency will make companies leaner. That’s actually something that seems to be accurate.

Profit margins for the S&P 500 have reached record levels, and earnings continue to be strong.

However, at least in the short term, the demand displacement from higher unemployment will take a toll on the economy.

If this happens, then the virtuous cycle of AI leverage will quickly reverse.

Enterprise Spend: The Numbers Don’t Lie

The whole AI story works, for now, because there are some very aggressive revenue projections baked into the narrative.

OpenAI believes it can reach more than $100 billion in revenue by 2029. But it’s already had to tamper with expectations in the last week. Increased competition from Anthropic has been cited as the culprit.

And that’s the issue. However big the AI market is, every day there are new entrants trying to gain market share while valuations keep increasing.

This disconnect will be felt eventually, much like it did in the dot-com bubble, no matter how revolutionary the technology is.

Semiconductors: Early Warning Sign

Semis are arguably the foundation of AI. They led the AI rally early on, and they have been on a historic streak in the last week.

Semis have made a new all-time high, and this could be a warning sign. Back in 1999, the SOXX also topped weeks ahead of the Nasdaq.

I would speculate that we might see AI froth rotate into safer tech, like the Mag 7, in the coming weeks or months, and this could lead to a similar divergence as what we saw in the early 2000s, just ahead of the historic market crash.

What Should Investors Do?

The problem is that nobody wants to be left out of the AI boom. It’s understandable. We have seen stocks increase 10x in a matter of months. But we’ve also seen plenty of stocks collapse too, and the environment today is more challenging than ever.

So here are my three tips to survive an AI recession.

Take profits when you can: While it sounds simple, it’s hard to let go of stocks that have run far. However, after two years of strong market gains, this is no longer the time to be a hero. Everyone is piling into AI, and many of these valuations and projections aren’t sustainable long-term.

Concentrate on a few high-conviction AI plays: This is important for two reasons. Firstly, knowing what you own will help you hold through the hard times and avoid panic selling. Secondly, by focusing on a few key areas, it’s easy to stay ahead of the market rather than just chasing everything.

For example, I'd argue that a company like TSM (TSM) remains a good long-term investment. Yes, it's had a big run, but it's essentially one of the few fabs out there.

On the other hand, I'd argue that a company like Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), which is both investing in AI and one of the main beneficiaries, is arguably still a great stock to own. It has great growth avenues beyond AI, including Waymo, YouTube, and a significant position in SpaceX (SPACE).

Ultimately, you want a company that still has a strong balance sheet, AI-independent cash flows, and doesn't require triple-digit growth to justify its valuation.

Find true diversification from AI: Which means diversification away from the main indexes like the S&P 500 and Nasdaq. Both of these are way too overexposed at this time.

Diversification can be found in select assets, like commodities, recession-resilient sectors, like utilities, or even international stocks.

In the event of an AI recession, credit is going to tighten, demand may collapse, and the companies that will do well will be those with strong balance sheets and revenues that can at least remain resilient in a downturn.

Utilities are always a good pick during recessions due to their stable cash flows, and I'd argue that many dividend-paying stocks with strong cash flows also fulfill this role.

A company like Brookfield Infrastructure (BIPC), for example, can provide stability in a recession while also giving you exposure to higher energy prices.

Or you can buy international stocks that are more uncorrelated to the U.S. markets, like emerging markets.

MercadoLibre (MELI) is one that is trading at a fairly reasonable price right now.

This isn't a stock that needs AI to thrive. Its tailwinds come from the economic growth of the area it serves.

Other Considerations

It's important to understand that while human nature has not changed, this technology is different.

Ultimately, I see a high likelihood that markets and investors will get ahead of themselves in terms of AI deployment and valuations, causing some level of reset.

However, we can't truly compare AI with anything we've seen before, and this makes predicting the top much harder.

Moreover, many of the main players here, the Magnificent 7, are companies with very strong cash flows and balance sheets.

This isn't going to be a wave of bankruptcies like in the dot-com era.

And this is why I still think some select exposure to AI is still prudent, and why I'd also be happy to buy the dip if we saw prices come down for many of these AI names.

Final Thoughts

Timing the exact top of the AI bubble is a matter of luck. However, we can make an educated guess as to where we are in the cycle.

AI has gone mainstream, investors are pouring into these stocks, companies are raising money at record levels, and no one is really thinking about the downside.

This is exactly the point when you want to be cautious.

Pay close attention to credit, jobs, and revenue projections, and make sure you're positioned accordingly once the tide shifts.

data.title: BridgeBio: Attruby's Early Dominance Makes The Bull Case Hard To Ignore
data.description:
Overview

During my last look in 2024, I rated BridgeBio Pharma, Inc. (BBIO) a “Buy,” but expressed some concern over competition and a limited cash runway. Pfizer (PFE) was already dominant in the ATTR-CM market with products like Vyndamax. And I thought that BridgeBio could benefit from an EU partnership or some sort of non-dilutive capital.

Be it from shuffling debt around or through the monetization of acoramidis in the EU via a royalty sale, BridgeBio has "kept the lights on." Acoramidis has also since been approved in ATTR-CM as “Attruby” in the U.S., and Pfizer is fighting a few generic drug makers who filed ANDAs challenging Vyndamax (tafamidis) patents.

The situation is a bit complicated for BridgeBio because although it competes head-on with Vyndamax, if tafamidis generics enter the ATTR-CM market earlier than hoped, it would weaken the Vyndamax/Attruby TTR stabilizer “duopoly” and lead to price erosion.
