In the article below, I propose that positive Attruby-related developments, alongside pipeline advancements, merit ongoing optimism in BridgeBio's stock despite its premium valuation amid ongoing losses and debt obligations.

Attruby

Pfizer just settled three major cases, with a negotiated generic entry date of June 1, 2031 (one, Apotex Corp., is outstanding). This is extended from what had previously been an anticipated 2029 cliff. This is “gospel" to the ears of BridgeBio and its investors because the ATTR-CM market remains a "protected branded space" through at least mid-2031, giving Attruby more time to establish its roots.

Already bearing fruit, Attruby generated $362.4 million in U.S. net product revenues last year. And BridgeBio estimates that Attruby accounts for over 25% of new-to-brand prescriptions.

Now, both BridgeBio’s and Pfizer's drugs are TTR stabilizers. Attruby was designed to have much better potency. BridgeBio has run with this differentiation in marketing language, citing Attruby as the “first and only approved product with a label specifying near-complete stabilization of TTR.” Since there are no head-to-head trials comparing the two, however, any "superiority" claim rests on cross-trial comparisons, which may not resonate with prescribers.

I did a DCF model on Attruby.

My assumptions include an 11% discount rate, or WACC. I have the drug peaking in 2031, with its loss of exclusivity year slated for 2039. In its recent annual filing, BridgeBio cited a target population of 50,000 in the U.S. Now, ATTR-CM is famously underdiagnosed, so I accounted for 3% annual population growth as diagnosis improves. Obviously, peak share assumptions are up for much debate, but I thought 18% was appropriate. It’s strong but also accounts for the "second-mover disadvantage." A $200,000 net price per year is 20% gross-to-net. COGS is at 8%. Launch opex is 50% for three years before settling at 30% steady-state opex. And, beginning in 2032, when tafamidis generics are expected to enter the market, I modeled for a 15% competitive impact (impacting market share assumptions).

I have Attruby generating just over $2.2 billion in peak annual sales, worth $5.46 billion in net present value. My estimate is on the conservative end of analyst estimates. If you do a Google search, you'll see that most estimates fall between $2 billion and $5 billion in peak annual sales. And although I lean conservative, as I usually do, I can certainly see the case for up to $4 billion in peak annual sales, which is why I also provided a sensitivity analysis in the image above.

For instance, if you bump up peak share assumptions to 22%, then the drug peaks at roughly $2.7 billion. Likewise, if I understated the annual population growth and it is, say, 5% instead, Attruby would peak near $3.5 billion. So, it goes to show that even small changes in key assumptions can have large impacts.

In any event, BridgeBio has an enterprise value near $15 billion, so this leads me to its pipeline in search of residual value.

Pipeline

Management likes to highlight three late-stage assets that could each represent over $1 billion in peak U.S. sales opportunities.

Infigratinib is differentiated because it is a once-daily pill. The market leader, BioMarin's (BMRN) Voxzogo, is a daily subcutaneous injection. But I think that a once-daily pill is a big competitive advantage in a pediatric population. Moreover, BridgeBio's PROPEL 3 study, which had data released in February, infigratinib showed a mean height velocity increase of plus 2.1 centimeters per year and also achieved the first statistically significant improvement in body proportionality, or the ratio of the upper body to the lower body. Mind you, Voxzogo nearly eclipsed $1 billion in sales last year.

Then we have encaleret. This one works by antagonizing calcium-sensing receptors to counteract the “gain-of-function” mutation in ADH1 patients that tells the body their calcium levels are high when they are actually low. Encaleret generated positive Phase 3 data in October: 76% of encaleret patients achieved target blood and urine calcium levels compared to just 4% on conventional therapy (i.e., high-dose calcium and Vitamin D). As BridgeBio’s patient estimates imply, this is a tiny market, though (25k US + EU). Encaleret's biggest opportunity is in chronic hypoparathyroidism (awaiting a Phase 2 trial initiation).

Finally, BBP-418 is positioned to become the first approved therapy for any form of limb-girdle muscular dystrophy after Phase 3 FORTIFY interim data earlier this year showed a rapid treatment effect. Patients completed a 100-meter walk 31 seconds faster than the placebo group after 12 months, and there were significant reductions seen in creatine kinase.

Financial Health

BridgeBio generated $502.1 million in revenue in 2025. About 25% of that came from "license and services" revenue. The company spent ~$452 million on R&D and ~$531 million on SG&A. Loss from operations last year was ~$523 million. The company ended 2025 with ~$587 million in cash and securities.

BridgeBio owed $855 million in deferred royalty obligations, $547 million in 2027 Notes, $741 million in 2029 Notes, and $565 million in 2031 Notes.

In January, management completed the issuance of $632.5 million aggregate principal amount of 2033 convertible Notes. This should cover the Notes due 2027.

Assuming similar cash burn, this gives them roughly two years of cash runway. From looking at consensus EPS and revenue estimates, BridgeBio is expected to become "EPS positive" in 2027.

Conclusion

Attruby is off to a rock-solid start in ATTR-CM, and Pfizer developments are bullish. Attruby should generate anywhere between $2 and $4 billion in peak annual revenue. In the interim, BridgeBio’s pipeline is compelling. I imagine there is at least another blockbuster or two in the near future. BridgeBio’s losses and debt obligations are worth watching, but I’m not too concerned. I think Attruby alone will power them to cash generation and allow them to deleverage (without sacrificing their “R&D engine”) within two to three years. So, I still see BBIO as a “Buy,” even near all-time highs.

Some risks to my “Buy” rating include regulatory setbacks (i.e., NDA rejections, label restrictions), competition in ATTR-CM (i.e., "second-mover disadvantage," GTN squeeze, Amvuttra, next-gen nucresiran, NTLA-2001) and achondroplasia (Ascendis's once-weekly injection, Yuviwel), clinical trial failures, operational overload (from potentially launching three new drugs within an 18-month window), and a shareholder-unfriendly capital structure (i.e., outstanding debt and royalty obligations).

data.title: CSRE: The Secret Sauce Of This Outperforming Active REIT ETF
data.description:
"Real estate remains one of the few areas in global capital markets where active management reliably creates value." --Sam Sahn, Portfolio Manager at Hazelview Investments

I've invested in real estate investment trusts ("REITs") since 2013, when my dad prodded me to pick a few stocks to buy in an attempt to help me learn about investing. I wasn't interested in investing at the time but bought a few stocks anyway. Two of those were REITs.

Years later, when I opened my brokerage account again, I was flabbergasted at what I saw.

My handful of stocks were not only higher in market price, they were also generating a steady stream of dividend income.

I understood that's how it works, in theory. But seeing it in real life was amazing. I've been an active investor of REITs ever since.

Lots of investors recognize the value of an allocation to REITs but lack the interest or time to be an active REIT-picker themselves.

That's where actively managed REIT ETFs come in.

A surprising number of active REIT ETFs have been launched in recent years. Among the best of those is the Cohen & Steers Real Estate Active ETF (CSRE), launched in early 2025.

Since inception, CSRE is virtually tied with the Janus Henderson US Real Estate ETF (JRE) for best performing active REIT ETF. Here are the two compared to four other actively managed REIT ETF peers:

  • Invesco Active US Real Estate ETF (PSR)
  • DFA Dimensional US Real Estate ETF (DFAR)
  • JPMorgan Realty Income ETF (JPRE)
  • ALPS Active REIT ETF (REIT)

CSRE has spent most of its existence as a publicly traded ETF outperforming its peers, but JRE has pulled ahead recently after a strong start to the year.

Interestingly, all 6 of these ETFs have outperformed the passively managed Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ) by anywhere from 1 to 5 percentage points since early 2025. Here's just the top two vs. VNQ for the sake of a cleaner illustration:

What's the secret sauce of these two actively managed REIT ETFs, especially CSRE?

Let's dig in to answer that question. Afterward, I'll discuss who active REIT ETFs might be right for and why I don't personally use them.

The Case For REITs

First, let's explain the bull case for commercial real estate in general. REITs tend to perform well when their underlying commercial real estate assets perform well.

In fact, REIT stock prices tend to move in advance of commercial real estate prices. And commercial real estate prices are heavily affected by long-term interest rates. While long-term interest rates have recently spiked due to the Iran war and high oil prices, the longer term trajectory still appears to be downward.

As such, while it could be delayed by the Iran war, commercial real estate broadly does seem to be entering a multi-year rebound into price appreciation.

Of course, "commercial real estate" is a very broad and diverse umbrella of various property types. The trajectory of multifamily may look very different than the trajectory of data centers.

But, generally speaking, the bulk of the CRE market moves in tandem with long-term interest rates.

Another bullish point supporting the CRE rebound is the supply outlook.

Across almost all sub-sectors, the development pipeline has shrunk considerably as a result of elevated capital and construction costs.

Due to tariffs on construction inputs as well as deportations that disproportionately impact the construction industry, construction costs continue to rise at a rapid pace.

This raises the hurdle rate to greenlight new CRE projects, and it also increases the gap between existing property values and replacement costs. Both are bullish for existing CRE properties.

Now, moving on to the bull case for publicly traded REITs.

If CRE enjoys a new multi-year trajectory of price appreciation, then so should REITs.

Plus, REITs on the whole continue to trade at a material discount to the broader stock market, although the valuation gap has narrowed somewhat this year.

While the average REIT discount to the broader stock market is about 0.6x, the current discount is about 3.6x.

Historically, when REITs have traded at such a considerable discount to the broader market, forward 1-year and 2-year returns have exceeded those of the broader stock market.

While it is impossible to say if past performance will translate into future performance, it is enough to say that the record of history is on REITs' side right now.

Active Outperformance Vs. Higher Fees

According to research from Hazelview Investments, actively managed REIT portfolios outperformed their passive index peers over a 15-year period of time, delivering 151 basis points of annual outperformance.

Additionally, the research found that actively managed strategies boasted superior risk-adjusted returns than passive strategies while achieving greater diversification across sectors and market cap sizes.

While passive real estate indices are heavily concentrated in large-cap, high-multiple REITs in the data center, senior housing, and industrial sub-sectors, actively managed ETFs diversify more heavily across real estate sub-sectors and market caps.

More so than the broader stock market, heavy indexing in the real estate sector has led to material inefficiencies that active managers can exploit.

Moreover, generalist stock investors tend to lack expertise in commercial real estate, while most experts in commercial real estate work in... commercial real estate. Relatively few investment professionals have deep proficiency in both commercial real estate and stock investing. That renders further inefficiencies in the realm of REITs.

As of 2024, actively managed real estate strategies outperformed passive real estate indices by 1-2% per year.

Compare that to most other actively managed stock funds, especially mid-cap and large-cap, that actually underperformed their passive benchmarks.

This is sobering data for the average stock-picker. If even most professional investors cannot outperform their passive index benchmarks, how likely is it that a retail investor will outperform a passive stock fund?

But the publicly traded real estate sector is a different story. Here, active managers consistently outperform.

Why?

The reasons given above play a big role. But also consider that Wall Street devotes fewer analysts and less resources to REIT coverage than for most other stocks.

That results in a wider dispersion between analyst consensus earnings forecasts and actual earnings results for REITs than for non-REIT stocks. (More analyst coverage generally renders a more accurate analyst consensus estimate.)

This, again, results in greater potential for mispricing of REITs than of the average non-REIT stock.

That gives active managers who specialize in commercial real estate and are skilled at fundamental analysis of REITs an advantage.

However, active REIT funds and ETFs also sport higher expense ratios and lower dividend yields than their passive peers.

Here's a quick comparison of VNQ to the 6 actively managed ETFs listed above:

ETF Expense Ratio 30-Day SEC Yield
VNQ 0.13% 3.6%
CSRE 0.70% 1.9%
JRE 0.65% 2.5%
PSR 0.35% 3.3%
DFAR 0.19% 2.7%
JPRE 0.50% 3.2%
REIT 0.68% 3.2%

VNQ has the highest yield and lowest expense ratio.

CSRE has the lowest yield and highest expense ratio.

Interestingly, the two best performing active ETFs since CSRE's inception also have some of the highest expense ratios.

So far, they have earned their higher fees through outperformance over VNQ, but it has come at the cost of a lower dividend yield. I am guessing that this is due to their exclusion of high-yielding value traps in the REIT space as well as their focus on the fastest growing REITs.

Still, that does diminish a lot of the appeal of owning REITs, which are designed to be income machines (though aren't always managed that way).

Making this an even tougher sell, REIT dividends are largely non-qualified, meaning that they are taxed at a generally higher rate than the mostly qualified dividends of non-REIT stocks.

Worse still, these actively managed REIT ETFs don't even outperform higher-yielding and lower-fee qualified dividend ETFs like the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD):

At least, they haven't outperformed SCHD for as long as they have existed as publicly traded vehicles.

But perhaps that will change in the coming years because of the bull case for commercial real estate and REITs outlined above.

CSRE's Secret Sauce

The first to note about CSRE is who manages it.

CSRE is issued by Cohen & Steers, which specializes in commercial real estate and other hard assets like infrastructure. C&S is the largest active REIT manager by assets under management.

Generally speaking, it would be sensible to opt for a management company/sponsor that specializes in a given space rather than one with no particular specialty.

Also note that CSRE is managed primarily by Jason Yablon, Head of Listed Real Estate at Cohen & Steers, who has analyzed REITs his entire career.

Of course, experience does not necessarily equate to skill.

So let's look at another Cohen & Steers fund for which Yablon is a portfolio manager: The Cohen & Steers Real Estate Securities Fund (CSDIX):

Sure enough, this mutual fund from C&S has outperformed the passive index, especially over the last decade.

And due to lower fees, CSRE has slightly outperformed CSDIX during its brief lifespan thus far.

As far as I can tell, Cohen & Steers imposes few rules on the managers of CSRE.

The team basically engages in bottom-up fundamental analysis to boil the roughly 150-stock investable universe down to their highest conviction picks.

As of the end of Q1 2026, that was 40 REITs. But even within this 40-REIT portfolio, the managers heavily concentrate the fund into their highest conviction picks. The top 10 holdings account for 58% of the portfolio.

While the passive, cap-weighted index allocates a ~10% weighting to leading senior housing REIT Welltower (WELL), CSRE allocates a roughly 50% greater weight to the REIT.

That's a common theme among actively managed REIT ETFs. JRE sports a ~12% allocation to WELL.

It's also clear that CSRE's managers are very bullish on technology-related real estate such as data centers and wireless communication towers, as these two sub-sectors together make up ~30% of the fund.

Other than what's stated above, it is hard to find more to say about this actively managed fund, because it isn't rules-based. It's up to the discretion of the portfolio managers.

Bottom Line

For investors who primarily aim for total returns (as opposed to income) and want to be as hands-off as possible, CSRE makes a fine option. In fact, it makes a strong argument for being the best option for exposure to REITs.

But the downsides of CSRE are hard to ignore. It has not outperformed most qualified dividend ETFs during its brief lifespan, and it offers a lower yield than most of those non-REIT ETFs as well.

Moreover, the 0.70% expense ratio will be a tough pill to swallow for many investors. A $10,000 investment in CSRE would cost $70 per year in fees. A $100,000 investment would cost $700 per year. Investors with a sizable nest egg can see those fees stack up quickly.

At High Yield Landlord, we seek to fill this gap by providing real-money, actively managed REIT portfolios overseen by managers with backgrounds in commercial real estate. And best of all, we don't sacrifice either quality or yield in our portfolio construction.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Meta Q1: Thanks For The Dip, I'm Buying More
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) was one of the most recent positions I've added to my portfolio (check it here), and I am satisfied with my decision so far. Even with a lot of news flow (check it here), both positive and negative, since the beginning of my position, my conviction in the long-term thesis has become even stronger.

After Q1 earnings, this became even more evident; I believe the market is still overlooking some positive points about Meta. I found the result solid, and even so the stock is falling in after-hours. The valuation was already good; with this decline, the margin of safety becomes even more obvious.

Meta’s Q1 Earnings

Let’s address the elephant in the room: Meta’s EPS was excellent and beat all estimates. We are talking about a $10.44 EPS in a single quarter, 62% growth. The market expected something closer to ~$6.6, so this was a huge surprise.

But getting straight to the point, this was non-recurring; it includes a huge (~$8 billion) tax benefit that made the effective tax rate -23%. So basically, it is not worth becoming optimistic based on this earnings beat.

However, I think it is possible to be optimistic about the rest of the indicators. Revenue grew an impressive 33% YoY, and this was also a beat by $760 million. With this very strong top-line momentum, operating income also performed well, growing 30%.

So we are still talking about a high-growth company, even ignoring the inflated EPS.

What makes me most optimistic is how Meta delivered this ~33% YoY revenue growth. YoY the number of users increased 4%, even with headwinds from Iran and Russia. Ad impressions increased a lot, growing 19%, and along with that, price per ad grew 12%. This creates an absurd compounding effect for the top line. We are talking about high ad growth, but revenue per ad also grew significantly, two components forming revenue that are in a very strong momentum, and that is why revenue is growing so much.

For me, this only reinforces that Meta’s core remains strong. I have had some concerns regarding the sustainability of continuing to increase users, or even continuing to increase ad impressions, since most of Meta’s platforms are already very mature. But Meta continues to prove that it can improve monetizable space, and that its initiatives allow its apps to become more profitable and also maintain or increase engagement levels.

One point I found more negative was margin. My expectation is that in the long term, Meta will be able to become more efficient and expand margin, even if gradually, through automation, pricing power, scale, and so on. But in this quarter, we saw that it is not delivering exactly this. But I do not see this as a problem because, besides the margin remaining flat, the biggest growth came from R&D expenses, which grew 46%. Whether we like it or not, this is the type of expense that allows revenue to continue growing, directly or indirectly.

Market Did Not Like Meta’s CapEx

Especially in the first hours, it is difficult to be certain about the reason for the market’s reaction. Maybe some funds/algorithms did not like the operating margin, or maybe they did not like the guidance for the next quarter. But I believe the two main factors, and maybe my opinion will change after fully listening to the earnings call, is that the market did not like CapEx.

In Q1, CapEx was $19.8 billion, below income from operations (although still a high number). But I believe the real issue is that guidance was increased once again. The company stated that now in 2026, capital expenditures should be in the range of $125 billion to $145 billion (previously the range was $115-$135 billion). So it was already at a high level, the market already did not like it, and now this expectation became even worse.

Indeed, for a company with income from operations around $22 billion, that is, something close to $90 billion annually, saying that capital expenditure can be $145 billion in one year is a lot. We should see strong pressure on free cash flow, possibly negative margins, increasing depreciation in the next quarters, possibly some level of debt or reduction in dividend/buyback payments. Basically, CapEx directly influences several numbers and expectations the market may have.

But I believe the uncertainty stops there; it is not something that affects the long-term thesis that much. Of course, if all this CapEx turns out to be useless, we would be talking about a horrible scenario where Meta could have distributed billions and billions to shareholders but burned it on initiatives that failed. But that would be the extreme scenario, perhaps worse than the Metaverse.

In practice, I believe many positive things will materialize from this infrastructure investment. I do not know what the ROIC will be, but I believe that in the long term, Meta will be able to extract positive growth to justify this type of investment, whether through new features, new revenues, or at least consolidating its platforms even further to maintain leadership in its core market.

Meta: Valuation And The Long-Term

Quarters are important both to see how the company is performing (beats and misses) and also to increase or decrease our long-term conviction. In this case, I believe Q1 earnings strongly reinforced how positive Meta’s long-term will be. As I mentioned in the first section, ad impressions and price per ad are Meta’s core, and the reported numbers were solid. Thinking exclusively about these two metrics, how will they look in 10 years?

Personally, I am optimistic. With so much investment in AI and mainly advances in this area, I believe Meta will continue to be able to create platforms with even higher engagement. For example, with AI, it is possible to further improve algorithms to deliver what people like, and it is also possible to encourage people to create more content and more quickly. This type of technology also accelerates new features for the platform. Threads is not showing strong engagement recently. Easier to analyze and easier to fix. All of this results in more people using and more people seeing ads.

The price per ad is also a trend that has been happening for some time. The better the algorithm, the better Meta becomes at delivering the right ad to the right person, in the right way, at the right time, and the more advertisers can pay. It is a win-win situation.

But beyond that, right on the first page of the earnings release, Zuckerberg highlighted the “first model from Meta Superintelligence Labs” and also mentioned that it is “on track to deliver personal superintelligence to billions of people.” I believe this creates an enormous level of optionality, and much of this comes from the massive investments Meta is making, which is precisely a part the market does not like very much.

Maybe you do not trust the business model of providing LLMs to consumers and charging a subscription for it, and indeed, it is not an easy business; CapEx is high, and competitors are very aggressive - Alphabet (GOOGL, GOOG), OpenAI (OPENAI), and Anthropic (ANTHRO). But Meta does not need to play exactly the same game. It already has strong distribution. If it can add more features to WhatsApp, which is used by billions of users, such as some AI to support WhatsApp business, that is already a large addressable market. If it can provide some service for content creators, that is another large addressable market. If this AI (such as Muse Spark) drives sales of Meta Glasses, that is another addressable market that is not only large but is also practically being created. So even if it is not possible to state that AI will generate $X billions of revenue for Meta by 2030, this type of investment creates so many possibilities that it makes me more optimistic and more convinced that in a few years Meta will have higher revenue, maintain strong growth, and also have more diversified revenue.

Regarding valuation, this is where Meta makes even more sense. After the ~6% decline, Meta’s market cap is $1.6 trillion. It seems high, right? But Meta just confirmed that it is a company capable of delivering $88 billion of operating income annually, disregarding growth. Just with these numbers, we can say there is a price-to-OI of ~18x currently.

But the point that really matters is growth. With this already low multiple and with such high growth, there is very attractive compounding. The market is estimating an EPS close to $36 for Meta in 2027. The P/E is 17x. If Meta can deliver 20% EPS growth per year until 2030, in a few years the P/E would drop to 10x (EPS would go to $62). 20% per year is a lot; it is not a base case assumption, but with so much investment, so much optionality, and a core with solid momentum, it is possible.

In this scenario not only shareholder yield would be high, but also the implicit upside. For instance, to be traded at only ~20x forward earnings, by 2029 we would need to see a 100% upside (with Meta stock at $1,244).

Bottom Line

Meta is not one of those cases where it is possible to have complete certainty. There are many regulatory risks that could cause eventual fines or even some loss of moat. There are also macroeconomic risks and/or changes in consumer preference that affect prospects for ad price growth and ad impressions. But even considering this, I think the market is underestimating Meta’s potential.

Uncertainty exists, but Meta’s moats also exist. Execution is good, and numbers are strong. All of this makes it possible to trust the long-term, and the good part is that since the scenario is not crystal clear, the valuation adjusts to that and offers an interesting margin of safety. Therefore, I maintain my Buy rating and I plan to increase my (small) position a little more at these prices.

data.title: ServiceNow: The AI Control Tower The Market Is Ignoring
data.description:
Software season is back with uncertainty as high as ever. ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW) has struggled in spite of delivering strong numbers, clear evidence of the poor sentiment in the software sector. Investors appear eager to dismiss near-term numbers due to fears regarding long-term AI disruption. I believe that this narrative underestimates the resiliency and importance of NOW’s platform as well as the company’s potential to offset that same narrative with efficiencies of its own. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.

NOW Stock Price

I last covered NOW in March, where I upgraded the stock to a strong buy rating amidst the SaaS apocalypse. The stock has fallen another 25% since.

The numbers paint a very different picture than that implied by the stock price. The company is executing on its AI opportunity, and the market continues to sleep.

NOW Stock Key Metrics

NOW is an enterprise software company that is famous for enabling digital workflows. Because workflows span vast use cases and the entirety of the business lifecycle, NOW has historically benefitted from a large and growing market.

That has culminated in strong financial results, with NOW being one of the rare software companies able to sustain aggressive top-line growth at scale. In the most recent quarter, NOW generated 22% YoY subscription revenue growth to $3.67 billion, slightly exceeding guidance of $3.653 billion. That beat, however, was rather small, and I suspect some investors may have interpreted this as a potential canary in the coal mine.

The company delivered solid growth in current remaining performance obligations (‘cRPOs’), which jumped 22.5% YoY. This, however, was slower than the 25% growth rate in the fourth quarter. I note that in my experience, cRPO growth has rarely correlated with forward revenue growth in spite of many apparently viewing it to be a reliable predictor of it. I suspect that some investors may have been hoping for an acceleration in both revenue and cRPO growth but were disappointed when they received neither.

NOW generated a solid 97% gross renewal rate in the quarter. This is a testament to how sticky the business is, indicating the minimal churn among customers (100% is the maximum result for this metric). Investors are concerned that this renewal rate may eventually fall if customers either replace enterprise solutions with cheaper alternatives (I view this to be an unlikely threat) or if customers use AI to reduce seats (this appears to be the primary driver behind the headwinds in the software sector). This will definitely be a critical metric to monitor to see if churn rises due to AI.

NOW did see its subscription gross margins decline roughly 300 bps in the quarter. Investors may be concerned that implementing AI may reduce the long-term profitability of the business.

Even so, NOW managed to post GAAP profits while also boosting non-GAAP operating margins by 100 bps to 32%, exceeding guidance of 31.5%. I expect profitability to be the main driving force behind any future software recovery.

It is curious to note that in spite of the bearishness surrounding potential AI disruption to its business, NOW has continued to increase its headcount. This appears consistent with management’s view that AI is a major tailwind for their business and an opportunity worth investing in.

NOW ended the quarter with $7.9 billion of cash and $1.7 billion of strategic investments versus $1.5 billion of debt, representing a strong net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to see around 22.5% YoY subscription revenue growth in the second quarter. Management slightly increased the full-year guide to see up to $15.775 billion in revenue, representing 22.5% YoY growth and up from the prior guide for $15.50 billion.

On the conference call, management noted that “50% of net new business now comes from a non-seat-based pricing model.” I expect this to be a high watermark among seat-based software peers. It is impressive that NOW has been able to shift towards usage-based pricing in such a dramatic way without it visibly affecting overall growth rates. Management did note that the conflict in Iran had some “potential impact to deal timing,” which appeared to have taken some investors by surprise. Nonetheless, management continues to view the company as being the “AI control tower” for its customers. The way I look at it, NOW has an opportunity to be the very thing that investors are afraid AI might do to it: be the orchestration layer for AI agents to implement work on other software solutions. Time will tell if management is able to rise up to the challenge.

Is NOW Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

There once was a time (technically less than a year ago) when NOW commanded healthy, premium valuations. Those days are long gone. While NOW still commands a premium relative to other software names, a lot of that looks justified given the company’s mission-critical positioning within its customer base. This is the kind of software that is central to business functioning and productivity. What doesn’t make a lot of sense is the stock valuation relative to growth rates, with the stock recently trading at just around 22x this year’s earnings.

Consensus estimates call for the company to finally see top-line growth decelerate below the magical 20% range over the coming years.

In my view, consensus estimates for profitability look quite understated. For instance, analysts expect NOW to generate around a 26.8% net margin by 2032. That would represent minimal margin expansion from the 26.2% estimate for this year. Sure, the company did indeed generate a paltry 18% GAAP incremental operating margin in the most recent quarter.

That said, I expect the company to post dramatically higher net margins over the long term, more in the 40% to 50% range. This would be due to AI-led efficiencies, which ironically form the backbone of the bear thesis. While bearish investors are concerned that NOW’s customers might use AI to reduce headcount, thus impacting seat-based pricing models, that might very well present the opportunity. Management appears focused on the long-term growth story, which of course remains important; however, for investors buying at these valuations, profitability appears to be the most visible catalyst. If we assume that NOW can generate a 40% net margin over the long term, then the stock is trading at around 14.5x long-term earnings. That multiple looks too conservative given that the top line is expected to grow at a healthy double-digit pace for a long time, alongside this remaining a highly resilient business. Perhaps the reader might wonder why I am so confident in a 40% net margin (or even higher). I note that mature companies like Adobe (ADBE) and Microsoft (MSFT) are already generating GAAP margins in the 30% to 40% range, and this is without the full force of AI. The prevailing bearish thesis centers around companies being able to replace human labor with AI agents. But software companies like NOW, in theory, would be able to undergo any such replacement with even greater intensity, given that these companies tend to have high fixed costs that appear to be well in the crosshairs of AI.

On the other hand, I expect NOW’s software to prove more resilient than peers, as I am doubtful that customers will be in a rush to replace the digital workflow provider, especially given that the company is investing aggressively to be a leader. These point to the potential for some multiple expansion - I see up to 50% potential upside if the stock trades up to a still-conservative 1.5x PEG ratio. If management delivers on their goals of becoming the AI control tower and delivers stronger growth than expected, then the upside may be even higher, but that is not needed at current valuations.

NOW Stock Risks

It is possible that NOW gets disrupted by AI. This might occur due to more drastic cuts to customer seats or perhaps AI enabling competitors to encroach on its market. It is possible that NOW engages in expensive M&A in efforts to change the narrative. It is possible that NOW loses its premium relative to other seat-based software vendors. It is possible that profit margins do not expand as expected, which might occur if NOW needs to lower prices to retain business.

NOW Stock Conclusion

I really do think that in due time, we will look back at this period with the understanding that while AI has indeed transformed how we work, the bearishness in software was dramatically overstated. NOW looks like a clear buying opportunity as it is successfully balancing between aggressively investing in its platform, delivering top-line growth, and expanding profit margins. The stock valuation is undemanding and offers significant upside potential. NOW is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.
